title: Why one model for many modalities

beats.text: A language model is a function from token embeddings to token embeddings, vectors in d-dimensional space, not characters or words.

visual.sub: the transformer attends to vectors, not text

beats.text: Self-attention mixes those vectors by relevance and has no built-in notion of text, so any d-dimensional token is fair game.

visual.note: the model only ever sees vectors in R^d

beats.text: If you turn an image into a handful of d-dimensional vectors, the transformer attends to them exactly as it attends to words.
So the dominant vision-language design is one transformer whose context holds image tokens and text tokens side by side.
Older systems were pipelines where a detector, a captioner, and a reasoner each discarded information and compounded errors.
The recipe is general: audio, video, depth, or sensor streams all become attendable once chopped into a sequence and embedded.
Images have no natural discrete units, so the Vision Transformer recipe manufactures them by cutting the image into fixed-size patches.
A 14 by 14 RGB patch is 588 raw numbers; one shared linear map projects it into a single d-dimensional patch token.

visual.note: the shared projection is a stride-P convolution in disguise

beats.text: A 224 by 224 image at patch size 14 gives 16 patches per side, so the encoder emits 256 patch tokens.
Projecting into width 4096 makes the map W a 4096 by 588 matrix, turning the image into 256 tokens of width 4096.
Token count scales as one over P squared, so halving the patch size quadruples the tokens and the attention cost.
Every patch token competes with text for the same context budget, so seeing more and reading more text are in direct conflict.
Three landmark systems define the design space, and almost every production vision-language model descends from one of them.

visual.sub: three ancestors of the modern VLM

beats.text: CLIP trains an image tower and a text tower on 400 million pairs with a contrastive objective that aligns vision with language.
The objective is a softmax cross-entropy that treats the matching caption as the correct class among all B captions in the batch.

visual.note: captions are the supervision; no labels needed

beats.text: Flamingo grafts a frozen vision encoder onto a frozen language model through newly inserted gated cross-attention layers.
LLaVA won on simplicity: project frozen CLIP patches with a tiny map and prepend them as extra input tokens to the text.
BLIP-2 added a Q-Former that learns query tokens to pull information from frozen vision into a frozen language model.
Everything reduces to one architectural fork: where do image tokens meet text tokens?
Early fusion concatenates image and text into one sequence so ordinary self-attention mixes both in every layer.

visual.note: one sequence of length N + T

beats.text: Its attention cost grows as N plus T squared, and the N image tokens are spent straight from the context budget.
Cross-attention keeps text as the only main sequence and lets it read into a separate image memory through inserted layers.

visual.note: cost N times T; text length and base LLM untouched

beats.text: Early fusion trades context budget for simplicity; cross-attention trades extra parameters for an unmodified, context-cheap LLM.
Query-based resamplers sit between them, compressing many patches into a small fixed set, though heavy compression can drop fine detail.
The modern LLaVA-style recipe trains in two stages on top of a pre-trained language model and a pre-trained vision encoder.
Stage one freezes both encoders and trains only the projection on image-caption pairs with the next-token objective.
Stage two unfreezes the projection and usually the LLM, fine-tuning on instruction data so the model follows instructions about images.

visual.note: loss over answer tokens only; image tokens masked out

beats.text: The standard suites probe distinct skills, from visual question answering and document reading to college-level multimodal reasoning.
Three caveats recur: benchmarks leak into training data, answer-matching is brittle, and hallucination is what accuracy hides.
A vision-language model that scores well on VQA can still confidently invent a clock on an empty wall.
